Gender Dynamics At Work: The Importance Of Workplace Culture
============================================================

[Download a PDF of this article](https://fticommunications.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/03/culture-at-work-infographic.pdf)

March 28, 2019

 Author: 

[](https://fticommunications.com/gender-dynamics-at-work-the-importance-of-workplace-culture/#)[](https://fticommunications.com/gender-dynamics-at-work-the-importance-of-workplace-culture/#)[](https://fticommunications.com/gender-dynamics-at-work-the-importance-of-workplace-culture/#)[](https://fticommunications.com/gender-dynamics-at-work-the-importance-of-workplace-culture/#)[](mailto:businessinquiries@fticonsulting.com)

[Corporate Reputation](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=corporate-reputation)[Diversity, Equity & Inclusion](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=d-e-and-i)[Employment Value Proposition](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=employment-value-proposition)[Organizational Culture](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=organizational-culture)[People & Transformation](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=people-transformation)[Workforce & HR](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=workforce-hr)[North America](https://fticommunications.com/category/insights/?term_slug=north_america)

**FTI Consulting and Washington, D.C.-based women’s leadership firm Mine The Gap today released the third installment of the firms’ research on gender dynamics in the workplace. This report, entitled [_Culture at Work_](https://gender.fticommunications.com/), highlights the critical importance of office and workplace cultures in cultivating, developing and protecting most companies’ biggest investment: their people.**

![Image 6](http://fticommunications.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Screen-Shot-2019-03-27-at-2.19.22-PM-768x972-1-237x300.png)FTI Consulting and Mine The Gap surveyed nearly 6,000 women and men professionals across the technology, finance, legal, energy and healthcare industries. According to the research, both women and men professionals, across all the surveyed industries, consider work-life balance and a welcoming office culture with camaraderie and connectivity among colleagues to be top corporate values, outweighing “financial perks” and “company growth.” For both women and men, “feeling undervalued” was the number one reason for leaving a company, above dissatisfaction with current compensation or being recruited away for better pay. In addition, 62% of women professionals reported personally feeling or witnessing others feel undervalued by their manager, team or company in the last year. Combined, these numbers demonstrate the need for workplace culture change in key industries to strengthen worker satisfaction and ensure long-term employee retention.

**Commenting on the research, Elizabeth Alexander, a Senior Managing Director in the Strategic Communications segment at FTI Consulting and a crisis communications and gender inclusion specialist**, said:

> “This data offers unique insight into a question plaguing companies across the country: why do our employees leave, and particularly, why are our women employees leaving? Our research belies a common myth that women leave their jobs solely due to family demands. Instead, the data demonstrates that the reasons for employee departures are much more nuanced, with a combination of problematic workplace cultures, unsatisfactory compensation and feeling undervalued by the company being the main drivers. To remain competitive and viable in today’s market, companies should carefully study their own workplace cultures, reconsider their own communications practices and make critical changes going forward, tailored to their employees’ needs.”

The Culture at Work survey also revealed significant gaps between professionals’ current workplace cultures and the office environments in which they believe they would thrive. The FTI Consulting/Mine The Gap research looked at four typical organizational cultures, identified by University of Michigan professors Robert Quinn and Kim Cameron: Clan, Adhocracy, Market and Hierarchy. The Culture at Work survey found that women and men overwhelmingly believe they would thrive in a “Clan” culture — a workplace built on teamwork, participation and consensus — while less than one-third stated they work at companies with such an environment. Many professionals also believe they would thrive in an “Adhocracy” culture, a dynamic and creative environment that promotes individual initiative and creativity; however, only about one-in-ten professionals reported that they currently work in this environment. Furthermore, while about one-in-three employees reported being in a workplace with a “Hierarchy” culture, defined by its focus on formalized structure and efficiency-based procedures, it was the lowest-rated culture in which both men and women say they would succeed. Approximately 1 in 5 women professionals report working in a “Market” culture, defined by its emphasis on results, stock and competition, while only 14% believe they would thrive in such a culture.

To download the full Culture at Work report and an accompanying infographic, please visit [https://gender.fticommunications.com/](https://gender.fticommunications.com/).

Gender Dynamics in the Workplace
================================

 Jul 13, 2023 

Share

*   [Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2023/03/24/gender-dynamics-in-the-workplace)
*   [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2023/03/24/gender-dynamics-in-the-workplace)
*   [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2023/03/24/gender-dynamics-in-the-workplace)
*   [Email](mailto:?subject=Sharing%20a%20link%20from%20the%20Vanderbilt%20Owen%20Graduate%20School%20of%20Management&body=https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2023/03/24/gender-dynamics-in-the-workplace)

Gender in organizations expert, Professor Kennedy, investigates how women can retain femininity while recognizing its worth and power

**_By Lacie Blankenship_**

[Jessica A. Kennedy](https://business.vanderbilt.edu/bio/jessica-kennedy/), Associate Professor of Management, is an expert on gender in work organizations; she is known for [her research](https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2019/01/23/professor-jessica-kennedy-shines-a-spotlight-on-ethics-gender-and-power-in-the-workplace/) at the intersection of gender, business, ethics, and power. Examples of Kennedy’s recent contributions include [a new study](https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2022/09/30/crossing-the-line-to-communicate-power/) showing that subordinate men are more likely to engage in sexually harassing behaviors, [busting the myth](https://business.vanderbilt.edu/news/2017/01/01/intellectual-capital/) that women are bad negotiators, and [shedding light](https://cdn.vanderbilt.edu/vu-web/owen/files/publications/Final_Kennedy%20Kray_Gender%20Negotiation%20ROB.pdf) on the unique advantages women bring into negotiations.

![Image 4: Vanderbilt Professor Jessica Kennedy ](https://cdn.vanderbilt.edu/vu-business/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/22093652/pjkennedy-300x267.png)
Professor Kennedy

In this article, Kennedy answers our questions on gender dynamics and how they are present in the workplace.

**What are gender dynamics?**
-----------------------------

Gender is the socially constructed meaning of biological sex. It’s one thing to be male or female, but another to create specific meanings out of biological differences. When our beliefs, expectations, and reactions to a person reflect patterns elicited by gender rather than something specific to the individual and his/her situation, the interpersonal interaction is informed by gender dynamics. For instance, when a man and a woman act identically assertive, but she is seen as less likable than he is, this is a gender dynamic. Or, when a man but not a woman elicits disgust for being a stay-at-home parent, this is a gender dynamic. Typically, gender dynamics refer to people treating men and women differently in ways that affect their power, status, and authority.

**How do gender dynamics present in the workplace?**
----------------------------------------------------

Organizations are intended to be highly rational places focused on completing tasks and achieving goals, with the most task- and socially-competent people in charge because it benefits the group. However, when we introduce gender dynamics, it’s a form of irrationality. Women might have a harder time achieving equal pay and authority despite displaying equal skill because gender dynamics distort people’s views of women’s skill and behavior. That pattern has been the focus of most research to date. However, it’s important to note that most of the things that are bad for women are also bad for men. Notions of masculinity have burdened men with a lot of financial pressure and very limited emotional permissions, in essence limiting their humanity and constricting their life opportunities in different ways.

**What should workplace leaders consider about gender dynamics?**
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Leaders drive culture by letting people know what is and isn’t acceptable behavior, so step one is making clear that the organization won’t tolerate disparate treatment of men and women at work. Generally, more formal, professional environments are better for women than informal ones. Educating people on what typical gendered expectations look like and what to do when they crop up is helpful too. When gender dynamics emerge, people typically think they are just reacting to the person; they don’t realize that gender informs their reaction, and that’s where knowing the research can help. If you know that women are punished more harshly for small errors, then you can watch for that, or if you know that women are more likely to be criticized when they are task- rather than relationship-focused, then you can call out that pattern and try to consciously correct if it comes up in a group discussion. I also think it’s worth noting that creating equal opportunity for people of identical talents has historically been a bipartisan goal. Too often in today’s polarized environment, DEI issues are seen as liberal concerns. But to have a stable society, we need to establish upward mobility and equal opportunity. Otherwise, hierarchies become oppressive and unstable.

**How do you expect workplace gender dynamics to change in the next 5 years?**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Gender categories are expanding, and the theories will have to expand to accommodate the new categories. We don’t know a lot yet about what it means to be, say, [non-binary](https://transequality.org/issues/resources/understanding-nonbinary-people-how-to-be-respectful-and-supportive). I think having people whose gender is more fluid and difficult to pin down will help to disrupt antiquated expectations. However, I’m not sure we have resolved the issue of femininity being devalued generally and in the workplace particularly, and I think more has to be discussed and done to eliminate that pattern. I think that [Kate Manne](https://philosophy.cornell.edu/kate-manne)’s work is insightful about the ways that women are expected to be like **The Giving Tree** in Shel Silverstein’s book, giving until they are a stump, so I plan to keep investigating how we can retain femininity while recognizing its worth and power.

**What advice do you have for people facing gender dynamics in their workplace?**
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I would recommend speaking up in a rational, curious, moderate way about what you see and what you need. For instance, if your review contained comments you thought were gendered, say so, in a respectful way, like, “When you said I am good with clients but not that technical, I was surprised because I did X, Y, and Z on the technical side this year.” Or if someone isn’t using your title, say, “I notice that you call Dr. X by his title but me by my first name. Why is that? I would prefer to go by my title too.” Don’t just sit and stew on the unfairness or vent to third parties. It’s great if a friend or mentor can speak up about the issue too, but we need to practice having difficult conversations and giving people the chance to learn from our perspectives. Retaining our voice can help maintain a sense of agency. Suffering in silence is the worst and will burn you out. Acknowledging the fear and anxiety caused by any historical experiences where we were treated unfairly can help keep the emotion levels moderate. We want to react to _this_ situation, not the sum of all our past experiences with the issue. That’s our responsibility, and why speaking up sooner is better. Yes, it feels risky, but that’s what genuine relationships require.

Speaking up with specific suggestions about how to change structures before issues arise is a great idea too. We can suggest solutions like asking all the job candidates identical questions in identical order, removing names from the resumes, or buying software to review for pay disparities. [One meta-analysis](https://journals.aom.org/doi/10.5465/amj.2013.0721) found that the gender pay and promotion disparity across situations was 14x larger than the performance disparity, so reward pay is really an issue.

When you face really overt bias, not just more subtle, unintended bias, then I say that it’s like an abusive relationship – don’t try to fix them; get yourself out, and take care to heal yourself by correcting false, negative beliefs about yourself that they might have instilled. I think it’s best to assume good intentions where you can, but when you have clear evidence to the contrary, don’t wait around letting discrimination sap your motivation. Move on before the situation gets too damaging.

Why Gender Diversity Matters in the Modern Workplace
====================================================

Estimated reading time: 3 minutes

While the working world has made plenty of strides with [gender diversity](https://aperian.com/blog/types-of-diversity-in-the-workplace/), we still have a long way to go in order to achieve true equality.

Consider these statistics:

*   Of the CEOs on the Fortune 500, [only 10.4% are women](https://fortune.com/2024/06/04/fortune-500-companies-women-ceos-2024/).
*   Women hold [less than one-quarter](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/topics/leadership/women-in-the-boardroom.html) (23.5% in 2023) of the world’s board seats.
*   In terms of median earnings, women, on average, [earn 12.2% less than men](https://www.oecd.org/en/data/indicators/gender-wage-gap.html?oecdcontrol-96565bc25e-var3=2023) in OECD (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) countries.
*   Globally, 40 million to 160 million women may need to move into [more skilled roles by 2030](https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/mckinsey/featured%20insights/gender%20equality/the%20future%20of%20women%20at%20work%20transitions%20in%20the%20age%20of%20automation/mgi-the-future-of-women-at-work-exec-summary-july-2019.pdf).
*   Per a report from Sheryl Sandberg’s LeanIn.org nonprofit organization, “[50% of men think women](https://business.linkedin.com/talent-solutions/blog/diversity/2017/11-stats-from-sheryl-sandbergs-gender-diversity-report) are well represented in companies where 10% of senior leaders are women.” 

The bottom line is this: [barriers continue to exist for women in the workplace](https://aperian.com/blog/attitudes-towards-women/), and organizations continue to search for impactful ways to make a change. Why? Evidence proves that gender diversity is a must for today’s companies, and increasing gender diversity helps fuel a high-performing and profitable organization.

Here’s why gender diversity matters in the modern workplace:

### **Gender diversity makes for profitable organizations.**

A McKinsey study entitled “[Diversity matters even more: The case for holistic impact](https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/diversity-and-inclusion/diversity-matters-even-more-the-case-for-holistic-impact)” shows some stunning correlations between gender diversity and profitability. Companies in the top quartile for gender and ethnic diversity in executive teams are on average 9% more likely to outperform their peers. Meanwhile, those in the bottom quartile for both are 66% less likely to outperform financially on average (up from 27 percent in 2020). This indicates that a lack of diversity is becoming more and more costly for organizations.

### **Gender diversity allows different voices to be heard.**

Without gender diversity, organizations threaten to miss out on a wide range of voices, opinions, life experiences, and skills. This range of different voices translates to outside-the-box thinking and the innovation that organizations need to succeed in the competitive modern landscape. As a [_Harvard Business Review_ article](https://hbr.org/2018/01/how-and-where-diversity-drives-financial-performance) points out, organizations with higher-than-average diversity rates have 19% higher innovation revenues. That “diversity of views” that gender diversity enables is a powerful driver for success.

### **Gender diversity supports a stable (and desirable) working environment.**

Nothing disrupts a company’s steady progress quite like job turnover—and statistics show that the more diverse a team, the likelier it is that an employee will stay in their position. [Glassdoor data](https://www.glassdoor.com/blog/diversity/) shows that more than 3 out of 4 job seekers and employees (76%) report that a diverse workforce is an important factor when evaluating companies and job offers. Additionally, [data from PWC](https://www.pwc.com/gx/en/about/diversity/iwd/iwd-female-talent-report-web.pdf) shows that 61% of women look at the gender diversity of the employer’s leadership team when deciding where to work. A focus on gender diversity not only helps you keep your trusted employees around—it also helps to attract the next wave of talent, too. Job seekers have a watchful eye out for companies’ diversity and workplace culture, and organizations with strong gender diversity and inclusion practices can set itself apart in the competitive modern job market.

### **Gender diversity creates better teams.**

Did you know that the new thoughts and perspectives gender diversity deliver to teams can boost teamwork and collaboration? Take a look at this research [from the University of Amsterdam](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=1826024), which states that teams with equal gender diversity helped ensure “mutual monitoring,” or job accountability, throughout—meaning a more successful outcome. Another [research study from Fortinet](https://www.fortinet.com/blog/business-and-technology/exploring-benefits-gender-diversity-cybersecurity) found that gender-diverse teams make better decisions 73% of the time versus 58% of the time for all-male teams.

### **Gender diversity expands your audience.**

The best way to speak and market to a specific customer demographic is through the representation and input of members of that demographic. Without the insight and knowledge gender diversity gives an organization, how can you be sure your marketing, sales, and product development are on the right track? Gender diversity helps open up your base of appeal—and in a business world where women, for example, account for [85% of all consumer purchases](https://girlpowermarketing.com/statistics-purchasing-power-women/)—it’s an absolute necessity.

URL: https://www.iawj.org/content.aspx?page_id=2507&club_id=882224&item_id=5240&pst=24181

SUMMARY:
The International Labour Organization (ILO) in 2011, in the document “Occupational Health and Safety from a Gender Perspective, Module for the Training of Male and Female Union Members,” points to the challenge of distinguishing between work-related stress and familial stress, particularly for women who face both, compounded by gender inequality and discrimination. Promoting a culture of gender equality in the workplace necessitates the support of local initiatives aimed at advocating for women's legal rights and enhancing the capabilities of civil society groups to champion women's involvement in governance. Enhancing gender equality in the workplace, particularly in areas vulnerable to political volatility such as maternity rights, gender pay gap, and sexual harassment, requires a nuanced approach that transcends traditional universalism.

Gender Equality in the Modern Workplace

 By renata maximiano O chaves

Posted: 4/25/2024, 3:49 PM

**The Future of Work: Empowering Gender Equality in the Modern Workplace**

**Introduction**

The employment protection scenario has seen considerable modifications as a result of important socio-political movements and the evolving public consciousness.The feminist movements and international organizations have achieved significant advancements and gained multiple triumphs. However, globally 10.3 percent of women live in extreme poverty today, and they are poorer than men. As reported by the United Nations in December 2023[[1]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn1),in the face of various interconnected problems, including geopolitical wars, violence, climate change, and environmental degradation, women persistently attempted to enhance global norms and standards across the economic, social, and political domains.

Over the last two decades, feminists have accomplished the task of identifying sexual harassment and defining it as a social problem[[2]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn2).Despite these efforts, sexual harassment of women persists. The revelations in the United States of the “Me Too” movement in 2017[[3]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn3) underscored the persistent and harsh realities that women have silently faced in the workplace. Similarly, numerous clerks [[4]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn4) courageously exposed instances of sexual harassment perpetrated by judges within the courtroom[[5]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn5).

In Brazil, a notable incident occurred in 2023 when a federal judge was forced into retirement following accusations of sexual harassment by over 87 women[[6]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn6). These cases illustrate the pervasive issue of violence within various workplaces, highlighting that even women in positions of relative privilege within the judiciary are not immune.

Feminist legal scholar and Michigan Law Professor Catharine MacKinnon believes that the #MeToo movement exposed “the disbelief and trivializing dehumanization of [sexual harassment] victims”.[[7]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn7) Combined with international initiatives promoting gender equality, the #MeToo movement has not only brought attention to the widespread issue of sex discrimination in workplace settings, it has also stimulated changes in legislation and policies designed to protect employees. These developments underscore the urgent need for ongoing efforts to extend best practices and maintain focus on legislative changes and civil society in order to achieve gender equality in workplace environments worldwide.

The concept of a "universal turn"[[8]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn8) in civil rights expands protections beyond rules that prohibit discrimination based on gender to rules of universal applicability. This represents a significant departure from earlier feminist legal reforms that were explicitly designed to protect women in the workplace. Civil rights scholars advocate for a broader, more inclusive framework that seeks to eliminate all forms of workplace mistreatment, regardless of the gender of the victim. This includes the anti-bullying movement's push to remove the "sexual" qualifier from harassment law, aiming to prohibit all forms of on-the-job discrimination.

The expansion of rights beyond gender issues reflects a holistic view of equality and includes the work-life balance movement's efforts to transcend the merely "family" leave policies to accommodate a wider range of life pursuits. Scholars advocating for universalism believe that pregnancy is just one type of personal choice, and employers should respect all workers’ decisions on how to balance life and leave. As Sima Bahous notes[[9]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn9) “…the backlash against gender equality is on the rise.”

The intent behind the universal turn—to create a more inclusive and equitable workplace for everyone—merits applause, nevertheless it also necessitates a cautious examination. The transition towards universal protection risks diluting the achievements made possible through decades of feminist advocacy and legal reform. An anti-harassment rule that prioritizes dignity, rather than gender, could undermine the impetus for courts, employers, and employees to consider how certain workplace interactions contribute to gender subordination[[10]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn10). Moreover, expanding work-family policies to work-life policies could trivialize the specific needs of caretakers and dilute protections such as parental paid leave not implemented yet even in developed countries. Furthermore,the adoption of universal norms may undermine the efficacy of civil rights safeguards by reducing the resources allocated to protect the most marginalized individuals.

This paper contends that excessively broad workplace protections to the point that distinctions among various types of discrimination become obscured poses a significant risk of undermining the unique challenges and inequalities that female workers face. The broad categorizations include the trivialization of sexual harassment as merely another form of workplace conflict and may lead to neglect of the specific needs surrounding maternity and family leave - areas where women have historically been disadvantaged in the labor market. Adopting a universal approach at this stage could present additional obstacles to achieving substantial gender equality in the workplace, particularly when fundamental principles have yet to be realized. Global crises, automation, and the on-demand economy disproportionately impact women, thereby slowing the pursuit of genuine equality in the workplace.

The purpose of this study is a demonstrate that the gender pay gap, sexual harassment, and health and wellbeing are crucial areas for legal intervention and reform. It also calls for caution against the indiscriminate adoption of universal workplace protections without a thorough consideration of their potential impacts on feminist legal achievements.

This study advocates for a nuanced approach that recognizes the value of universal protections for fostering an inclusive workplace while also preserving the specific legal and policy advancements that address the distinct forms of discrimination and harm experienced by women. This balanced perspective is essential to ensure that the pursuit of broad-based protections does not inadvertently perpetuate the very inequalities it aims to eliminate.

Two aspects of this study should be kept in mind. First, although forms of discrimination may be intentional or unintentional, this study focuses only on the outcome of the discrimination; intent is not a factor. This focus ensures an objective analysis of the effects irrespective of their underlying motives. Second, it is important to differentiate between the terms “sex” and “gender”. While “sex” refers to the biological distinctions between males and females, “gender” encompasses the attributes that are socially and culturally constructed. The significance of gender identity is further elucidated in the Advisory Opinion issued by the Inter-American Court of Human Rights in 2017[[11]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn11) which advocates for a nuanced interpretation that integrates both biological and social elements.

The manuscript is organized into four defined sections, each designed to discuss the relevant topics. Part I initiates the discussion by examining the ongoing gender pay gap and its underlying factors, elucidating the structural obstacles that perpetuate economic inequalities. Part II explores the persistent discrimination and disparities prevalent in the workplace, with a particular emphasis on sexual harassment. Part III examines the distinct effect of workplace challenges on women’s health and well-being, analyzing the intersections of these issues with professional life and their implications on overall health outcomes. Part IV offers a compendium of legal remedies and policy recommendations tailored to effectively tackle the challenges delineated throughout the analysis, providing concrete, actionable steps for stakeholders at various levels to implement meaningful change.

**I- Addressing the Wage Gap for Gender Equality**

Although notable advancements have been made in women’s economic participation, political representation, formal employment, and educational achievements, the gender pay gap continues to be a widespread characteristic of labor markets globally. Empirical data consistently reveals that, on average, women earn 23%[[12]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn12) less compensation than their male counterparts.

The phenomenon of the global gender pay gap serves as a manifestation of enduring disparities between males and females across society and places of work. The societal and cultural conventions that assign men to decision-making roles and women to caregiving responsibilities have a substantial impact, not only on the type of paid job that women are directed toward but also on how that work is regarded and compensated.

Notably, only a portion of the pay disparity can be attributed to differences in the qualifications and attributes of women and men[[13]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn13). This suggests that the majority of the wage gap cannot be solely explained by variations in professional experience, education, or skill sets, thereby underscoring the existence of systemic inequalities in pay structures.[[14]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn14) These systemic pay gaps indicate enduring inequalities, leading to significant lifetime income disparities for women and increasing their risk of poverty.[[15]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn15)

The disparity in equal pay underscores a significant gap between the advancements in women’s opportunities and ongoing pay inequalities, casting doubt on the effectiveness of existing strategies to bridge this economic divide. To understand a fundamental cause- the gender pay gap- it is essential to examine employment gender segregation. This segregation occurs when distinct occupations and sectors disproportionately divide men and women, contributing significantly to gender inequality and the pay gap. In the workplace, these disparities are often categorized as horizontal and vertical segregation.[[16]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn16)

Horizontal gender segregation is observed when a particular gender predominates in certain occupations. This phenomenon is evident in industries such as construction, where men significantly outnumber women, and in sectors like public administration, health, education, and care, which are predominantly staffed by women. Such segregation not only limits the diversity of the workforce within specific fields but also reinforces stereotypical notions about "gender-appropriate" roles.[[17]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn17)

Vertical gender segregation, on the other hand, manifests as the underrepresentation of gender in positions at the higher end of a ranking system, which is often based on factors such as income, prestige, and job stability. This type of segregation highlights the challenges faced by women in climbing the occupational ladder within any sector due to the "glass ceiling" [[18]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn18)– a term denoting the visible and invisible barriers that hinder women's advancement to leadership and decision-making roles[[19]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn19).

This form of sex discrimination, which contributes to wage disparity, presents significant detection challenges, and its legal recognition remains ambiguous, complicating efforts to address and rectify such disparities. The gender pay gap reflects the sexual division of labor designed by the patriarchal structure of the current capitalist system, which assigns women secondary roles in the workforce.[[20]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn20)

The concept of “equal pay for work of equal value” is essential for achieving gender equality. The International Labour Organization’s Equal Remuneration Convention (ILO)[[21]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn21), goes beyond the idea of paying the same person for the same job to embrace a wider standard that assesses work of similar value based on qualifications, abilities, responsibilities, and working conditions. The standard of equal value allows for the acknowledgment of work across different roles, especially in roles traditionally held by women.

The principle of equitable compensation for work of equivalent value transcends mere economic or legal matters—it is also a significant declaration of society's acknowledgment of the equal value of each person's effort. This is an important step in reversing the patriarchal systems that have traditionally determined the conditions for economic involvement, providing a path to a fairer and more equitable labor marketplace.

Maternity-related discrimination significantly contributes to the gender pay gap. Legislative efforts aimed at achieving workplace equality for women have sought to dismantle the systemic barriers imposed by gender discrimination.

Despite International Labour Organization Conventions 111 and 183 challenges persist, notably in developed countries such as the United States, where there is no mandate for paid maternity leave. The Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA), the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), and the Pregnancy Discrimination Act (PDA), protect against discrimination but do not offer substantial labor market protections for women, underscoring the necessity for mandated paid leave.

The recent Pregnancy Workers Fairness Act mandates reasonable accommodations for any period of incapacity including pregnancy-related conditions, yet it does not guarantee paid maternity leave, highlighting the inadequacy of current anti-discrimination protections.[[22]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn22) Moreover, under the FMLA, pregnancy, as opposed to incapacitation due to pregnancy is not considered a “serious health condition” within the meaning of the FMLA. Under the Pregnancy Discrimination Act (PDA) an employer is required to treat pregnancy as any other temporary disability[[23]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn23), this situation exemplifies how universal protections can perpetuate disparities in the workplace.

In contrast, Brazilian legislation provides a strong framework designed to protect women from conception through postpartum, serving as a potential model that could inspire other countries. The Constitution[[24]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn24) prohibits the dismissal of pregnant women without just cause, mandates a maternity leave of 120 days- typically starting 28 days before the expected childbirth date, with provisions for special circumstances during pregnancy[[25]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn25). Furthermore, the legislation safeguards the rights of adoptive mothers by granting them maternity leave, based on the principle of equality, ensuring that adoptive and biological children receive equal care and rights.[[26]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn26)

Career interruptions due to maternity and caregiving responsibilities disproportionately affect women, impacting their professional development[[27]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn27). These interruptions not only impact the accumulation of their work experience but might also influence their willingness to engage in continuous professional development to maintain or enhance their skill sets. Consequently, women are less likely to pursue education and training that is directly aligned with market demands, anticipating potential career breaks, which in turn affects their earning potential. Such reduced experience and skill levels contribute to lower earnings throughout a woman's career[[28]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn28).

Furthermore, women might select jobs or industries that offer the flexibility needed to juggle their unpaid caregiving duties with their professional roles. Traditional norms and gender stereotypes, prevalent in family settings, educational institutions, and workplaces, significantly influence the career choices of both women and men.

All these factors might be thought of as the “maternity penalty”[[29]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn29) and they amplify the present wage disparity, as employed mothers experience reduced remuneration, a disparity that intensifies with the number of children a woman has[[30]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn30). There exists a correlation between diminished remuneration for mothers and several factors, including decreased working hours, engagement in family-oriented occupations that often offer lower income, hiring and promotion options that disadvantage the professional careers of mothers, and an absence of programs focused on facilitating women's reintegration into the workforce following periods of unemployment.

Feminists can ground arguments for prioritization of caregiving in the state's duty toward dependent or vulnerable citizens, rather than the notion of maternalism simply valuing women's roles. Pauli Murray argues strongly for the concept of maternity leave as a form of service for society, “maternity legislation is not sex legislation; its benefits are geared to the performance of a special service much like veterans’ legislation”[[31]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn31).

Europe[[32]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn32) and Brazil[[33]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn33) demonstrate progress in achieving workplace equality by providing paid parental leave to both mothers and fathers. Additionally, there is a growing trend to further improve paternity benefits [[34]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn34). This evolutionary development indicates an important step in achieving gender equality[[35]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn35), highlighting the collaborative endeavor necessary from the international community to address the wage disparity which is a fundamental indicator of human rights and gender equality.

The successful application of affirmative action, equitable compensation for work of comparable value, family pay leave, and adequate structure for estimating the value of employment, should be characterized by transparency, fairness, and flexibility in response to the changing dynamics of the labor market. This is the pathway for gender equality. Systemic reforms of this nature not only hold the potential to promote gender equality inside the workplace but also demonstrates a wider dedication to social justice and human rights.

**II- Sexual Harassment as a Barrier to Gender Equality in the Workplace**

Sexual harassment in the workplace remains a pervasive barrier to gender equality, impacting not only the victims but also the overall culture and productivity of organizations.

Existing national and workplace mechanisms should be extended or adapted to effectively prevent and manage violence and harassment in the world of work.

The consequences of sexual harassment extend beyond the immediate discomfort and emotional strain it causes. Victims often experience long-term career repercussions, psychological trauma, and deteriorating physical health.

Over 140 countries have enacted legislation aimed at addressing workplace violence and harassment in recent years, yet there continues to be a critical need for the rigorous enforcement of these laws, accountability for offenders, and preventive measures,[[36]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn36)including occupational safety and physical health management systems, policies, and programmers. These prevention mechanisms should be updated to enable protection against all forms of violence and harassment by responding to the different needs of the people.

Statistics reveal that over 20% of individuals have been subjected to physical, psychological, or sexual harassment at their place of employment[[37]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn37). The problem is intricately linked to power imbalances within workplace hierarchies.

Global research conducted by the International Labour Organization (ILO) of the United Nations found that harassment does not uniformly impact women but is more prevalent against the more vulnerable[[38]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn38). Vulnerability may be a result of class, age, race, color, marital status, or simply, because of fear of losing one's job.

The risk of violence and harassment at work increases when these characteristics intersect. Young women report incidents of sexual violence and harassment at twice the rate of young men, and migrant women are nearly twice as likely to experience such issues compared to their non-migrant counterparts[[39]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn39). Worldwide, young women in employment (ages 15–24) were most likely to have experienced violence and harassment at work within the past five years, with a prevalence of 23.3 % [[40]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn40).

Overall, women represented the majority of those experiencing sexual violence and harassment, and they were slightly more likely than men to have faced psychological violence and harassment. Sexual harassment which creates a hostile or offensive environment for members of one sex is a central arbitrary barrier to sexual equality in the workplace. Discriminatory harassment is a legacy of women's historical development.

A significant barrier to eliminating sexual harassment in the workplace is the frequent impunity of perpetrators, rooted in the challenges of substantiating allegations of misconduct. Direct evidence is exceedingly rare, necessitating that plaintiffs often rely on circumstantial evidence to contest the legitimacy of the defendant's general denial. Frequently, the circumstances include "me too" testimony, where plaintiffs utilize the experiences of similarly situated employees to demonstrate patterns of discrimination [[41]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn41). This method has emerged as a potent strategy for plaintiffs to overcome the significant legal barrier necessary to demonstrate their claims[[42]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn42) and influenced judicial perspectives on workplace discrimination.

Statute limitations are another challenge for the survivors. Statistics shows[[43]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn43) that the prevalent characteristic of the offender is a person enjoying a position of authority or power, therefore victims may fear repercussions for complaining about the harassment, and such fear may explain a delay in opposing the conduct[[44]](file:///C:/Users/pando/Downloads/Renata%20Maximiano%20O%20Chaves%20Article.docx#_ftn44). For a victim in a vulnerable position, rallying her internal resources and support structures to raise a complaint of sexual harassment against a person in dominance is likely to take some time.

Achieving gender equality in the workplace necessitates concerted efforts to eliminate sexual harassment. These efforts must extend beyond the implementation of laws and policies and encompass a transformative shift in workplace culture to actively support victims and hold perpetrators accountable. A commitment to these principles is essential for creating safe and equitable work environments for all individuals.

Organizations struggle with gender biases, unequal pay, and retention issues, which are exacerbated by workplace cultures that undervalue contributions of diverse employees.
